world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Open exploration of real-world locations in fantasy reimaging; ruins intact but mysterious; normal environmental presentation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Free movement through areas; enemy encounters in dungeons; NPCs in home locations; quest progression available

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Celestial object visible; ominous sky changes; increased ruined/hazardous environment visibility; Dark Gaia shadow looming
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Urgency in NPC dialogue; restricted return to certain areas; new transformations becoming available; boss encounters intensifying

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Comet transforms into explicit dark entity form; Tower of Babel fully accessible; ultimate arena visual manifestation; light vs. dark visual contrast
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final boss battle; all character forms usable; Mystic Statue artifacts activated; climactic narrative event

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Modern school setting in final scene; environmental reset to normalcy; character repositioning in contemporary context; visual break from fantasy ruins
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPCs interact as if in normal contemporary life; memory loss reflected in character behavior; world returned to natural progression

environmental_storytelling.example: Great Pyramid, Great Wall of China, Angkor Wat, Incan ruins, Nazca Lines serve as dungeons; players learn world history while progressing
Abandoned explorers and empty-handed returns mentioned in setting; dungeon hazards suggest why previous expeditions failed
16th-century maritime adventure framing; references to Christopher Columbus; treasure-seeking motivation established through environmental design
Mystic Statues scattered across ruins; each artifact piece unlocks Tower of Babel secrets; physical world objects encode narrative progression
Dark Spaces serve dual purpose as save points and story moments; Gaia encounters reinforce guardian role; transformation ability locations mark narrative progression
Comet's revelation as Blazer War weapon; previous approaches visible through evolutionary disruption; past cataclysm shapes current world state

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Flute-based techniques; non-combat focus; area-access abilities; investigation-oriented movement
Melee attacks; lower damage output; shorter reach than Freedan; health bar depletion on damage
Sword attacks; higher damage than Will; extended reach; combat specialization visible in increased melee effectiveness
Pseudopod attacks; highest damage output; unified manifestation; ultimate power display
Aggressive movement; health bar display; room-based patrol patterns; drop gold spheres upon defeat
Respawn upon re-entry; resume attack patterns; no repeat bonuses awarded for clearing same room twice
Cannot be revisited once defeated; unique visual presentation; story-critical encounter; single defeat permanent
Provides guidance; enables character form switching; facilitates healing; stationary protective presence
Contemporary school environment behavior; retained friendship bonds despite lost adventure memories; suggests unconscious connection

notes: Illusion of Gaia employs visual communication through real-world location placement (environmental framing), comet progression mechanics (time pressure visualization), and character transformation gating (ability-based world access). Danger is communicated through ancient ruin hazards and comet approach; safety through Dark Space sanctuaries and Gaia presence. Faction identity is subtle—no explicit warring factions, but rather primordial forces (Blazer War legacy) vs. current world protection (Gaia). The game uses world-state progression tied to comet arrival timeline, with visual environmental shifts from exploration-phase safety to climactic confrontation. Memory erasure ending complicates traditional faction loyalty by erasing factional memory entirely, suggesting ephemeral narrative agency. Limited source data; interpretations based on single Wikipedia article covering plot and gameplay mechanics.
sources_used: wikipedia: Illusion of Gaia article (gameplay section, plot section, setting/story details)
